Facts.text: John III ends his reign as Catholic Pope
Hunger appeal by women of Utrecht
Battle of Gravelines: In France, Spanish forces led by Count Lamoral of Egmont defeat the French forces of Marshal Paul des Thermes at Gravelines
Dean of St. Paul's London, Alexander Nowell, perfects a way to bottle beer
Haarlem surrenders after 7 months to Spanish army
Battle at Roundway Down: Royalists beat parliamentary armies
Aleksei Romanov succeeds his father Michael as Tsar of Russia
Van Marco Cesti's opera "Il Pomo d'Oro" (the Golden Apple or Tomato) premieres in Vienna
Treaty of Constantinople establishes peace after Russo-Turkish war

US Congress adopts the Ordinance of 1787 (Northwest Ordinance), establishing the first organized US territory, setting requirements for statehood, guaranteeing equal status with the original 13 states, outlawing slavery, and protecting civil liberties
Battle of the Vosges between French forces and those of Prussia and Austria
US Patent #1 is granted for locomotive wheels after 9,957 unnumbered patents
John Loudon discovers tin on East Indian Island of Billiton
In the Battle of Guaymas, Mexico, General Jose Maria Yanez stops the French invasion led by Count Gaston de Raousset Boulbon
US forces shell and burn San Juan del Norte, Nicaragua
Battle of Corrick's Ford, Virginia (Carrick's Ford): Union army takes complete control of Western Virginia
Battle of Murfreesboro, fought in Rutherford County, Tennessee begins (Forrest's Raid), Confederate victory (US Civil War)
Anti-draft protests in New York City turn violent, with over 100 people killed, including the lynching of Black individuals, 2,000 injured, and 50 buildings burned; riots last for 3 days
Battle of Bayou La Fourche, Louisiana
Battle of Tupelo, Mississippi (Harrisburg) [->July 15]
Rebellion at Morgan's, Ohio [->July 26]
Early retreats from Washington City back to Shenandoah Valley
Horace Greeley, founder and editor of the "New-York Tribune," reputedly advises his readers to "Go west, young man"
Oscar J. Dunn, former slave, installed as Lieutenant Governor of Louisiana
World's first championship cat show is organized by Harrison Weir and held at Crystal Palace, London
29th US Postmaster General: James N. Tyner of Indiana takes office
Congress of Berlin ends, redrawing the terms of Treaty of San Stefano to give Russia less influence in old Ottoman Empire
Train derails near Tcherny, Russia, killing 200
Germany Army Bill is ratified
Philadelphia outfielder Ed Delahanty becomes second major leaguer to hit 4 HRs in a game as Phillies lose 9-8 to Chicago Colts at the West Side Grounds, Chicago
San Francisco Ferry Building at foot of Market St opens
Phillies beat Pittsburgh 23-8
Great Britain and Japan renew their alliance of 1902 for another 4 years, which is the reason Japan joins WWI on the Allies' side
Vision of the Virgin Mary appears to children of Fátima, Portugal
Boston Red Sox pitcher Carl Mays walks off mound blaming teammates for lack of support in field
Race riots in Longview, Gregg County, Texas
American explorer Roy Chapman Andrews discovers the first recognized dinosaur eggs in the Gobi Desert, Mongolia
Albin Stenroos wins Olympic marathon (2:41:22.6)
French occupation force begins evacuating country
David Sarnoff reports in the NY Times, "TV will be a theater in every home"
First football World Cup competition begins in Uruguay
US-Russian commerce treaty takes effect
112°F (44°C) is recorded in Mio, Michigan (state record)
114°F (46°C) is recorded in Wisconsin Dells, Wisconsin (state record)
Kroller-Muller museum opens in Holland
Eddie Mayo (LA-Pacific Coast League) spits in face of umpire Ray Snyder
World War II: Montenegrins start popular uprising against the Axis Powers (Trinaestojulski ustanak).
5,000 Jews of Rovno Polish Ukraine, executed by Nazis
German occupiers imprison 800 prominent Dutch as hostages
SS shoots 1,500 Jews in Josefov Poland
Vilnius, Lithuania, liberated
René Pleven forms French government
Great Flood: 2,000,000 acres in Kansas and Missouri in central US are flooded by rain-swollen Missouri and Kansas Rivers
The Battle of the Kumsong River, the last major battle of the Korean war, begins
Dean Stone receives credit for an AL win, although he doesn't retire a batter, as he throws out Schoendienst trying to steal home, AL-11 NL-9

Beaux Arts Trio (Menahem Pressler, piano; Daniel Guilet, violin;Bernard Greenhouse, cello) make their debut at the Berkshire Music Festival (now Tanglewood Music Center), Lenox, Massachusetts, United States
The last execution of a woman in Britain, Ruth Ellis, takes place at Holloway Prison, London
WCBI TV channel 4 in Columbus, MS (CBS) begins broadcasting
KDBQ-AM in San Francisco CA changes call letters to KYA
500 Indonesian parachutist land on New Guinea
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Indian government announces it will cut last remaining links with South Africa by refusing landing facilities to South African aircraft
A. C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada founds the International Society for Krishna Consciousness (Hare Krishna movement) in New York City
Race riots break out in Newark, New Jersey killing 27
French government Couve de Murville forms
Russia launches unmanned Luna 15 to the Moon
Building of Amsterdam metro begins
The annual "Twelfth" parades pass off without serious incident in Northern Ireland
A series of gun-battles and shootings erupt across Belfast between the Provisional Irish Republican Army and British Army soldiers
NFL franchises LA Rams (Irsay) and Baltimore Colts (Rosenbloom) swap owners
Hector de Campora resigns as President of Argentina
India's first one-day international against England at Headingley
8.5 inches (21.6 cm) of rainfall in Dover, Delaware (state record)
Court martial begins in USSR for Valery Sablin (Hunt for Red Oct)
Last day of Test Cricket for English all-rounder Brian Close at age 45
NYC experiences a 25-hour blackout
Albania drops diplomatic relations with People's Republic of China
Alexander Ginzburg sentenced by Soviet court to 8 years
Ford Motor Company chairman Henry Ford II fires company president Lee Iacocca
Jockey Ron Turcotte (36) aboard Flag of Leyte Gulf, crashes during a race at Belmont Park, crushing several vertebrae and remaining paralyzed from the waist down until his death in 2025
Russian dissidents Ginsburg, Piatkus, and Sjtsjaranki sentenced to work camps
Walter Poenisch completes a swim of 207 km from Cuba to Florida
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Iran launches first attack into Iraq
Train crash in Aalter, Belgium, kills five
The Transvaal Attorney General announces that Eugène Terre'Blanche, leader of the far-right Afrikaner Weerstandsbeweging (AWB), and three associates will face terrorism charges, South Africa
Sergei Bubka of USSR pole vaults a record 5.89 m
The last sitting of an all-white Parliament in South Africa
Live Aid concerts held at both Wembley Stadium (London) and JFK Stadium (Philadelphia, Pennsylvania) raise over $70 million for African famine relief
Zola Budd and Annette Cowley are banned from the Commonwealth Games, a direct consequence of Britain's refusal to support economic sanctions against the apartheid government of South Africa
Red Sox replace manager John McNamara with Joe Morgan
Bob Milacki and three other Baltimore Oriole pitchers no-hit A's 2-0
The Tripartite Alliance, consisting of the African National Congress, South African Communist Party and Congress of South African Trade Union, outlines a mass action plan for August
Space shuttle STS-70 (Discovery 20) launches
Cigar wins a record 16th consecutive race, tying Citation's record from 1948
Indonesian ferry sinks, killing at least 77
International Olympic Committee votes to award Beijing the 2008 Summer Olympic Games
TV police procedural "The Closer" debuts on TNT starring Kyra Sedgwick
19th ESPY Awards: Dirk Nowitzki and Lindsey Vonn win
Mumbai is rocked by three bomb blasts during the evening rush hour, killing 26 and injuring 130
19-30 people are killed after a train collides with a truck in Malelane, South Africa
China's economic growth drops to 7.6%, its lowest level for three years
Financially troubled Scottish football club, Rangers, is voted into the third division
#BlackLivesMatter created in response to the acquittal of George Zimmerman on trial for the murder of Trayvon Martin
A bus crashes in Minas Gerais, Brazil, killing 10 people
A gravel truck collides with a bus in Podolsk, Russia, killing 18 people and injuring 40
Greek Credit Crisis: Eurozone agrees conditional deal to lend €86 billion over 3 years if Greece passes reforms
Sandra Bland is found dead in Waller County Jail, Texas, after spending the weekend in jail following a traffic offense; her family disputes her supposed suicide
Chelsea FC sacks high-profile manager Antonio Conte despite winning the 2016-17 EPL and 2018 FA Cup and replaces him with ex-Napoli boss Maurizio Sarri
Johnson & Johnson ordered to pay record $4.7 billion in damages in talc cancer case by jury in US state of Missouri
Outline of 5,000-year-old henge is discovered at Newgrange, Ireland, through drought and drone footage
Suicide bombing in Baluchistan, Pakistan, targeting election campaign event kills 128; Islamic State claims responsibility
US Department of Justice charges 12 Russian intelligence officers with cyberattacks against Democratic officials during 2016 US election

After the Atlantic League-MLB partnership rule changes, Southern Maryland Blue Crabs' outfielder Tony Thomas becomes the first player in professional baseball history to steal first base in a 7-2 win against the Lancaster Barnstormers
Unprecedented early fire season in western US as 67 large fires and nearly 918,000 acres are reported burned by Interagency Fire Center
Sri Lankan President Gotabaya Rajapaksa flees to the Maldives just before leaving office amid nationwide protests and economic unrest

Former US PresidentDonald Trumpsurvives an assassination attempt at an outdoor campaign rally in rural Butler, Pennsylvania; gunman Thomas Matthew Crooks fires 6 to 8 shots killing one spectator, critically wounding two, and hits Trump in the ear with a bullet before being killed by a Secret Service sniper
Evian Championship Women's Golf, Evian Resort GC: Australian Grace Kim claims her first major trophy with a miraculous second playoff hole win over Atthaya Thitikul of Thailand
FIFA Club World Cup Final: Chelsea defeats Paris Saint-Germain 3–0, becoming the inaugural world champions under the expanded FIFA format
William I of Scotland, key rebel in Revolt of 1173-1174, captured at Alnwick by forces loyal toHenry II of England
Oliver Cromwellconstrains English army leader John Lambert
CaptainJames Cookbegins his second voyage to the South Seas aboard HMS Resolution to search for Terra Australis (Southern Continent)
Source of Mississippi River discovered by American geographerHenry Schoolcraft
Swedish-American inventorJohn Ericssonfiles for a patent for his screw propeller design
Queen Victoriais first monarch to live in present Buckingham Palace
P. T. Barnum's museum in New York burns down, giantess Anna Haining Swan just escapes
KingWilhelm Iof Prussia sends the "Emser Depeche (Ems Telegram)" toOtto von Bismarckwho publishes an edited version to purposely offend the French government precipitating the Franco-German War
Finnish runnerPaavo Nurmisets world record 3000 m (8:20.4) in Stockholm, Sweden
Babe Ruthhits 700th career home run against Detroit
Richard Straussresigns as chairman of Reichsmusikkammer (Nazi Reich Chamber of Music)
Frank Sinatramakes his recording debut, with The Harry James Orchestra, singing "From the Bottom of My Heart" and "Melancholy Mood"
PopePius XIIexcommunicates communist catholics
Doctors operate and remove seven bone fragments from MLB Boston Red Sox playerTed Williams' elbow following injury suffered 2 days earlier in All-Star game
IndustrialistCyrus Eatonfirst offers to host a conference on dangers of nuclear weapons in Pugwash, Nova Scotia
RCA releasesElvis Presley's single "Hound Dog," a cover of Big Mama Thornton's original, written by Jerry Leiber and Mike Stoller, backed with "Don't Be Cruel," written by Otis Blackwell
US Democratic convention nominatesJFKas presidential candidate
Future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcherEarly Wynnwins his 300th and final MLB game at 43

Northern Ireland loyalistIan Paisleyaddresses a crowd at Loughgall, County Antrim, and is reported to have said, "I am anti-Roman Catholic, but God being my judge, I love the poor dupes who are ground down under that system."
Bobby Murcer's three homers account for all RBIs, beating KC 5-0
BBC bansSex Pistols' "No One is Innocent"
Calif'sNolan Ryanand Boston's Steve Renko each lose no-hitters in 9th
George Harrisonreleases single "Faster"
British guitaristJeff Beckquits singer Rod Stewart's tour after seven shows
Eddie Van Halenmakes a guest appearance, performing "Beat It" at a Jacksons concert in Dallas, Texas
Doctors at Bethesda Naval hospital remove two small benign polyps from US PresidentRonald Reagan's colon
New York Yankees retireRoger Maris(9) andElston Howard(32) uniform numbers
Federal judge throws outBette Midler's $10 million suit against Ford Motor Co, which used a sound-alike voice for its TV commercials
Kylie Minoguereleases her debut single "Locomotion"
Stingperforms his first Rainforest Fund benefit concert at the Kennedy Center in Washington, DC
Former NFL running back, broadcaster and actorO.J. Simpson(charged with murder) gives hair samples for testing
Tonya Harding's ex-husband Jeff Gillooly sentenced to 2 years in prison for attack on American Olympic figure skaterNancy Kerrigan
Pedro Martínezstrikes out the first four batters in the All-Star Game at Fenway Park, earning All-Star Game MVP
UK singer-songwriterLily Allenreleases her debut album "Alright, Still" (Grammy nominee Best Alternative Music Album)
Brewing companyInBevannounces deal to buy American brewer Anheuser-Busch for almost $52 billion
"She Wolf," the lead single fromShakira's eighth album, premieres
FIFA World Cup Awards:Lionel Messi(Golden Ball), James Rodriguez (Golden Boot), andManuel Neuer(Golden Gloves)
FIFA World Cup Final, Maracanã Stadium, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil: Mario Götze scores an extra-time winner as Germany beats Argentina 1-0
Theresa Mayis elected Prime Minister of the United Kingdom by Conservative Party MPs
"Dunkirk," a film directed byChristopher Nolanwith ensemble cast including Mark Rylance and Tom Hardy, premieres in London, England
US PresidentDonald Trumparrives in Paris for a 2-day visit with French PresidentEmmanuel Macron
ActressScarlett Johanssonwithdraws from a transgender film role after criticism of her casting
HBO announces that it has obtained the rights to "The Nevers," a project byJoss Whedon
Large protests in London against US PresidentDonald Trumpfeaturing Trump-like baby blimp asPresident TrumpmeetsQueen Elizabeth IIat Windsor Castle
Kawhi Leonardundergoes surgery to repair a partial tear of the ACL in his right knee
Violent protests and looting following the jailing ofJacob Zumaleave 337 dead in South Africa, with more than 200 shopping malls set on fire
US PresidentJoe Bidenlands in Israel to begin a four-day trip to the Middle East
19th-century explorerMatthew Flinders, who first circumnavigated Australia and named it, is reburied in Donington, Lincolnshire, the village of his birth
The Hollywood Sign is officially dedicated in the hills above Hollywood, Los Angeles. It originally reads "Hollywoodland," but the last four letters are dropped after renovation in 1949.
John Dee, English alchemist, astrologer and mathematician, born in London, England (d. 1609)
Arthur Dee, English physician, born in Mortlake, London (d. 1651)
Clement X [Emilio Altieri], Italian Pope (1670-76), born in Rome (d. 1676)
Václav [Wenceslaus] Hollar, Bohemian-born etcher (depictioned17th century London), born in Prague, Bohemia (d. 1677)
Ferdinand III, King of Hungary & Bohemia and Holy Roman Emperor (1637-57), born in Graz, Duchy of Styria (d. 1657)

Robert Calder, Scottish British Royal Navy admiral (Seven Years' War; Napoleonic Wars), dubiously court-martialed after Battle of Cape Finisterre (1805), born in Elgin. Scotland (d. 1818)  [Note: some sources cite birthplace as Kent, England; O.S. birthdate  July 2]
Alexander Balashov, Russian general and statesman, born in Moscow (d. 1837)
Wilhelm Heinrich Wackenroder, German writer (Fantasies about Art), born in Berlin (d. 1798)
Caroline of Baden, Queen of Bavaria, born in Karlsruhe, Margraviate of Baden (d. 1841)
Pellegrino Rossi, Italian economist and politician, born in Carrara, Duchy of Massa and Carrara (now Italy) (d. 1848)
John Clare, English poet (Poems Descriptive of Rural Life and Scenery), born in Peterborough, Northamptonshire, England (d. 1864)
James Alexander Seddon, politician and lawyer (Confederate Secretary of War, 1862-65), born in Falmouth, Virginia (d. 1880)
Gustav Freytag, German writer and playwright (Debit and Credit), born in Kreuzburg, Silesia (d. 1895)
Stanislao Cannizzaro, Italian chemist (Reaction of Cannizzaro), born in Palermo, Kingdom of the Two Sicilies (d. 1910)
Otto Wagner, Austrian architect and leading figure in the Vienna Secession movement, born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1918)
George Bradley, American baseball pitcher (first no-hitter in MLB history 1876; NL ERA leader 1876), born in Reading, Pennsylvania (d. 1931)
Stewart Culin, American ethnographer, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1929)
Sydney Webb, English socialist and economist (founded London School of Economics) and husband of Beatrice Webb, born in London, England (d. 1947)
Emma Mary Woolley, American educator and women's rights activist (President of Mount Holyoke College), born in South Norfolk, Connecticut (d. 1947)
Karl Erb, German opera and oratorio tenor, born in Ravensburg, Kingdom of Württemberg (d. 1958)
John Francis Larchet, Irish arranger (Irish national anthem), composer (By the Waters of Moyle), and musical director (Abbey Theatre - Dublin, 1908-35), born in Sandymount, Ireland (d. 1967)
Jean Murat, French actor (Eternal Rain, Carnival Flanders), born in Périgueux, Dordogne, France (d. 1968)
Louise Mountbatten, Queen of Sweden as consort of King Gustaf VI Adolf, born at Schloss Heiligenberg, Seeheim-Jugenheim, Germany (d. 1965)
Stan Coveleski, American Baseball HOF pitcher (World Series 1920; AL ERA leader 1923, 25; AL strikeout leader 1920; Cleveland Indians, Washington Senators), born in Shamokin, Pennsylvania (d. 1984)
Franco Casavola, Italian composer, music theorist, and futurist, born in Modugno, Italy (d. 1955)
Isaak Babel, Russian writer, playwright and journalist (Red Calvary) [OS Jun 30], born in Odessa, Russian Empire (d. 1940)
Sidney Blackmer, American actor (played Ted Roosevelt in 7 movies, Rosemary's Baby), born in Salisbury, North Carolina (d. 1973)
Mordecai Ardon, Israeli painter, born in Tuchów, Austria-Hungary (d. 1992)
Julius Schreck, German Nazi military man (1st head of the SS), and confidant of Adolf Hitler, born in Munich, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1936)
Martin David, German-Dutch law historian and papyrologist, born in Poznan, German Empire (d. 1986)
George Lewis [Joseph Zenon], American jazz clarinetist and bandleader (Bunk Johnson; Preservation Hall Jazz Band; "Burgundy Street Blues"), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1968)
Eric Portman, English actor (Naked Edge, Canterbury Tale), born in Yorkshire England (d. 1969)
Mickey Walker, American boxer (World Welterweight Champion 1922-26; World Middleweight Champion 1926-29), born in Elizabeth, New Jersey (d. 1981)
Kenneth Clark, English art historian (Civilisation), born in London, England (d. 1983)
Alfredo M. Santos, Philippine's first four-star General of the Armed Forces, World War II hero, born in Manila (d. 1990)
Harry Sosnik, American orchestra leader (Jack Carter Show, Your Hit Parade), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1996)
Charles H. Zimmerman, American aeronautical engineer (NACA, NASA), born in Olathe, Kansas (d. 1996)
David Branson, British piano prodigy, composer, musicologist, and visual artist, born in King's Lynn, Norfolk, England (d. 1987)
Paul Constantinescu, Romanian composer (Ballad of the Outlaw for cello and orchestra), and pedagogue, born in Ploiești, Romania (d. 1963)
Souphanouvong, President of Laos (1975-87), born in Louangphrabang, Laos (d. 1995)
Washington Castro, Argentine cellist, conductor, composer, and pedagogue, born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 2004)
Hines Johnson, West Indian cricket fast bowler (3 Tests; first WI fast bowler to take 10 wickets in a Test), born in Kingston, Jamaica (d. 1987)
Dave Garroway, American TV host (Today Show, 1951-61), born in Schnectady, New York (d. 1982)
Mærsk Mc-Kinney Møller, Danish shipping magnate, born in Hellerup Denmark, (d. 2012)
Walter Landor [Landauer], German-British industrial and brand designer, and consumer researcher, born in Munich, Germany (d. 1995)
Tex Hill, American World War II fighter pilot and triple flying ace (Flying Tigers, US Army Air Corps), born in Gwangju, Korea, Empire of Japan (d. 2007)
William F. Quinn, American lawyer, politician (1st Governor of the State of Hawaii, 1959-62), and businessman (CEO of Dole Pineapple Company, 1965-72), born in Rochester, New York (d. 2006)
Anna Halprin, American dancer and choreographer (Five Legged Stool, Parades and Changes), born in Wilmette, Illinois (d. 2021)
Hans Blumenberg, German philosopher (metaphorology), born in Lübeck, Germany (d. 1996)
Charles Scribner IV, American literary publisher, born in Quogue, New York (d. 1995)
Ernest Gold [Goldner], Austrian-born American film composer (Exodus), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1999)
Friedrich Peter, Austrian far-right politician, born in Attnang-Puchheim, Austria (d. 2005)
Git Gay [Birgit Carp], Swedish revue director, actress, and singer (Git Gay Show; Ge mig en lektion i kärlek), born in Karlshamn, Sweden (d. 2007)
Anker Jørgensen, Danish politician, Prime Minister of Denmark (1972-3, 75-82), born in Copenhagen (d. 2016)
Lois Kibbee, American actress (Edge of Night), born in Rhinelander, Wisconsin (d. 1993)
Sudie Bond, American stage and screen actress (Love Story; Johnny Dangerously), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1984)
Carlo Bergonzi, Italian operatic tenor, born in Vidalenzo, Italy (d. 2014)
Karl Pelgrom, Dutch sculptor who was committed to public art (The Pig, Sea Lions), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1994)[1]
Simone Veil, French politician (first female President of the European parliament 1979-82), born in Nice, France (d. 2017)
Al Rex [Piccirilli], American rock upright bass player (Bill Haley & His Comets - "Rock Around The Clock"), born in Norristown, Pennsylvania (d. 2020)
Bob Crane, American DJ and actor (Hogan's Heroes; The Donna Reed Show), born in Waterbury, Connecticut (d. 1978)
Donal Michalsky, American composer, born in Pasadena, California (d. 1975)
Leroy Vinnegar, American jazz and session bassist (Shelly Manne; Teddy Edwards; Les McCann), born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 1999)
Tommaso Buscetta, Sicilian mafioso and informant, born in Palermo, Sicily (d. 2000)
Bill Moor [William Hendrickson Moor III], American actor (Kramer vs. Kramer, The Devil's Advocate, Hanky Panky), born in Toledo, Ohio (d. 2007)
Antonio Roma, Argentine football goalkeeper (42 caps), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 2013)
Hubert Reeves, Canadian astrophysicist, and scientific writer, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2023)
Per Nørgård, Danish composer (Papalagi; Babette's Feast), born in Gentofte, Denmark (d. 2025)
David Storey, British novelist and playwright (This Sporting Life), born in Wakefield (d. 2017)
Patsy Byrne, English actress (Stealing Heaven, Blackadder II), born in Ashford, (d. 2014)
Aleksei Yeliseyev, Soviet cosmonaut (Soyuz 5, 8, 10), born in Zhizdra, Soviet Union
Antonio Jiménez Quiles, Spanish road cyclist (Vuelta a España 1955 runner-up), born in Granada, Spain (d. 2023)
Gordon Lee, English soccer manager (Port Vale, Blackburn Rovers, Newcastle United, Everton, Preston NE, KR Reykjavik), born in Cannock, England (d. 2022)
Peter Gzowski 'Mr Canada', Canadian broadcaster, writer and reporter, born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 2002)
Roger Reynolds, American Pulitzer Prize-winning composer (Whispers Out of Time), born in Detroit, Michigan
Jack Kemp, American NFL player and politician (Rep-R-NY 1971-89, US Secretary of Housing 1989-93, VP candidate), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2009)
Qiu Xigui, Chinese paleographer known for his work on Chinese oracle bones, born in Shanghai, China (d. 2025)
Albert Ayler, American jazz saxophonist, bandleader, and composer (Spititual Unity; The Hilversum Session), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1970)
Cal Ramsey, American basketball broadcaster, small forward (NY Knicks), born in Selma, Alabama (d. 2019)
Charles Coody, American golfer (US Masters 1971), born in Stamford, Texas
Myroslav Skoryk, Ukrainian contemporary classical composer (Carpathian Rhapsody), and pedagogue, born in Lwów, Polish Republic (d. 2020)
Paul Prudhomme, American Cajun chef (K-Paul), born in Opelousas. Louisiana (d. 2015)
Diethelm Ferner, German soccer midfielder (2 caps; SV Werder Bremen, Rot-Weiss Essen) and manager (Rot-Weiss Essen, Hannover 96, Schalke 04, Lebanon), born in Kragau, Germany (d. 2023)
Grahame Corling, Australian cricket fast bowler (5 Tests, 12 wickets, BB 4/60; NSW CA), born in Newcastle, Australia
Jacques Perrin, French actor (Cinema Paradiso, 317th Platoon) and filmmaker, born in Paris, France
Lut Tomsin, Belgian actress and director (Knock on the Door), born in Tongeren, Belgium
Robert Forster [Foster], American actor (Medium Cool, Jackie Brown), born in Rochester, New York (d. 2019)
Steve Gibbons, British singer-songwriter, guitarist, and record producer (The Ugly's - "Wake Up My Mind"; solo - "Tulane"), born in Harbourne, Birmingham, England
(Jim) "Roger" McGuinn, American folk-rock guitarist, and singer-songwriter (The Byrds - "Turn! Turn! Turn!"; "Eight Miles High"), born in Chicago, Illinois
Jay Uzzell, American R&B singer (The Corsairs - "Smokey Places"), born in La Grange, North Carolina (d. 2009)
Rod Chandler, American politician (Rep-R-WA, 1983-93), born in La Grande, Oregon
Stephen Jo Bladd, American drummer (J Geils Band - "Centerfold"; "Freeze Frame"), born in Boston, Massachusetts
Tom King, American rock guitarist, songwriter, guitarist, and arranger (The Outsiders - "Time Won't Let Me"), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2011)
Carlo Tavecchio, Italian sports executive (President Italian Football Federation 2014-17), born in Ponte Lambro, Italy (d. 2023)
Danny Lockin, American actor and dancer (Hello Dolly), born in Lanai, Hawaii (d. 1977)
Leroy Hodges, American soul and R&B bassist (Hi Rhythm Section; Al Green), born in Germantown, Tennessee
Eric Freeman, Australian cricket fast bowler (11 Tests, 34 wickets, 2 x 50s; South Australia), born in Adelaide, Australia (d. 2020)
Ashley Mallett, Australian cricket spin bowler (38 Tests, 132 wickets, best 8/59; South Australia), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 2021)
Jean-Pierre Plooij, Dutch writer and playwright (Duvelsmoer verhalen; Patience; Bird House), born in the Netherlands (d. 1994)
(Richard) "Cheech" Marin, Chicano-American comedian (Cheech & Chong), actor (The Golden Palace; Nash Bridges), and art collector, born in Los Angeles, California
Andrew Pryce Jackman, British progressive rock keyboardist, arranger, and composer (The Syn), born in England (d. 2003)
Bob Kauffman, American basketball forward (3 x NBA All-Star; Buffalo Braves) and coach (Detroit Pistons 1977-78), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2015)
Rodney Mills, American mastering engineer (The Doobie Brothers; Pearl Jam; Gucci Mane), born in Douglas, Georgia
Alf Hansen, Norwegian rower Olympic gold, double sculls 1976; World C'ship gold double sculls 1975, 78, 79, 82), born in Oslo, Norway
Daphne Maxwell Reid, American actress ( The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air, 1993-96; Frank's Place), born in New York City
Michel Maïque, French rugby league second rower (8 Tests; FC Lézignan) and politician (Mayor of Lézignan-Corbières 2014-20), born in Aude, France (d. 2021)
Robert A. Underwood, American politician (Rep-D-Guam), born in Tamuning, Guam
Ronald Machtley, American politician (Rep-R-Rhode Island), born in Johnstown, Pennsylvania
Tony Kornheiser, American sports commentator (Pardon the Interruption; The Tony Kornheser Show) and journalist (Washington Post, 1979-2008), born in New York City
George Nelson "Pinky" American astronaut (STS 41C, STS 61-C, STS-26), born in Charles City, Iowa
Ma Ying-jeou, Taiwanese politician (President of the Republic of China, 2008-16; Chairman of the Kuomintang (KMT), 2005-07 & 2009-14; Mayor of Taipei, 19998-2006), born in Hong Kong
Didi Conn [Bernstein], American actress (Denise-Benson, Grease), born in Brooklyn, New York
Rob Bishop, American politician (Rep-R-Utah 2003-21), born in Kaysville, Utah
Alla Pavlova, Russian contemporary classical composer (The Old New York Nostalgia Suite; Miss Me ... But Let Me Go), born in Vinnitsa, Ukraine SSR, Soviet Union (now Ukraine)
Gil Birmingham, Native American-Spanish actor (Yellowstone, Hell or High Water, Wind River), born in San Antonio, Texas
Milica "Mila" Mulroney (née Pivnički), wife of former Canadian prime minister Brian Mulroney, born in Sarajevo, PR Bosnia-Herzegovina, FPR Yugoslavia.
Violeta Dinescu, Romanian pianist, composer (Tabar), and music professor, born in Bucharest, Romania
Danitra Vance, American comedienne (SNL, 1985), and actress (Jumpin' At The Boneyard), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1994)
David Thompson, American NBA guard, 1975-84, 4X All-Star (Denver Nuggets, Seattle Supersonics), born in Shelby, North Carolina
Louise Mandrell, American country singer (Mandrell Sisters), born in Corpus Christi, Texas
Rick Chartraw, American NHL ice hockey defenseman, 1974-84 (Montreal Canadiens, Los Angeles Kings and 2 other teams), born in Caracas, Venezuela
Sezen Aksu [Yıldırım], Turkish pop singer-songwriter ("Şımarık" - "Kiss Kiss"), born in Sarayköy, Turkey
